content:
The biggest difference between SQL and DynamoDB isn’t how indexes work; it’s why they exist. Once you understand that, partition keys, GSIs, and access patterns all start to make sense.

10 min read
Just now

--

When “Just Add an Index” Stops Working
If you’ve spent a few years building backend systems, you’ve probably heard this sentence more times than you can count.
“The endpoint is slow.”
Almost immediately, someone asks:
“Do we have an index on that column?”
It’s practically muscle memory for SQL developers.
At one company I worked with, a dashboard endpoint that listed customer orders started timing out after a major sales event. The feature itself hadn’t changed. What had changed was the data.
The orders table had quietly grown from a few hundred thousand rows to tens of millions.
Suddenly, a query that once finished in milliseconds was taking seconds.
The first question during debugging wasn’t:
“Should we redesign the database?”
It wasn’t:
“Should we migrate to another database?”
It wasn’t even:
“Should we shard the database?”
It was simply:
“Is there an index on customer_id?”
And honestly, that’s exactly the right instinct.
Because in the world of relational databases, indexes exist primarily to make existing queries faster.
Most of us have solved performance issues this way dozens of times.
In fact, that’s probably why DynamoDB feels confusing at first.
You open the documentation and see terms like Global Secondary Index (GSI) and Local Secondary Index (LSI).
Your brain immediately translates them into familiar territory.
“Got it. These are just SQL indexes with AWS names.”
That assumption feels reasonable.
It’s also the biggest reason many engineers struggle with DynamoDB.
Because while SQL and DynamoDB both use the word index, they’re solving completely different problems.
And that’s where our story begins.

Why SQL Needs Indexes
One of the reasons relational databases have stood the test of time is that they let you think about your business first, not storage.
Imagine you’re building the backend for an e-commerce platform.
Your orders table might look something like this.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

orders tableThe schema makes sense because it mirrors the business.
An order belongs to a customer.
A product belongs to an order.
Payments relate to orders.
Shipments relate to orders.
That’s how relational databases encourage you to think.
Relationships first. Storage details later.
Now imagine a customer opens your app and taps “My Orders.” Your backend executes something like this.
SELECT *
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 42
ORDER BY order_time DESC;When the table has a few thousand rows, life is good. The query is fast. Nobody thinks about indexes.
Then success happens. Your application grows. A Black Friday sale, a viral campaign, or simply a few years of business later, your orders table contains 50 million records.
The exact same query now behaves very differently.
Not because SQL suddenly became bad.
Because finding a handful of rows inside tens of millions of records takes work.
Think about it for a moment. If someone handed you a spreadsheet with 50 million rows and asked you to find every order placed by Customer 42, what would you do?
Without any additional information, you’d start at row one. Then row two. Then row three.
Eventually, you’d find the matching rows. That’s essentially what a table scan is.
The database reads rows one by one until it finds what you’re looking for. It’s correct. It’s reliable. But it’s doing far more work than necessary.
This is where indexes enter the picture.
Suppose instead of searching the entire spreadsheet, someone also gave you a separate page that looked like this.
Customer 15 → Rows 8, 19, 55
Customer 42 → Rows 102, 891, 1450
Customer 87 → Rows 11, 900…
Now you don’t need to inspect every row. You jump directly to the relevant ones. That’s exactly what a SQL index provides.
It’s simply an organized structure that helps the database find existing rows much more efficiently.
The data hasn’t changed. The way the database reaches the data has.
Your application code barely changes. Your schema barely changes. Yet a query that took several seconds suddenly completes in milliseconds.
If you’ve worked on production systems long enough, you’ve probably seen this happen.
A new dashboard is launched. Everything looks great during testing. A few months later, support tickets start appearing.
“The Orders page is taking 12 seconds to load.”
Engineering investigates. The API code hasn’t changed. Infrastructure looks healthy. CPU isn’t maxed out.
The culprit??
A query that has quietly transitioned from scanning thousands of rows to scanning millions.
The fix??
Often, it’s a well-designed index.
A SQL index doesn’t change your data. It gives the database a much faster route to the rows that already exist. That’s why adding an index can feel almost magical. Your application barely changes, yet a query that took seconds suddenly finishes in milliseconds.
💡 Key Takeaway
SQL indexes don’t change how data is stored.
They change how efficiently the database reaches it.
That idea has shaped how generations of backend engineers think about performance. And honestly, it’s a good mental model……until you meet DynamoDB.
Because DynamoDB doesn’t start with the question:
“How can I make this query faster?”
It starts with a very different question:
“How do I organize my data so this query is possible at massive scale?”

The SQL Mindset: Design First, Optimize Later
One of the reasons relational databases have remained the default choice for decades is that they let developers focus on the business before worrying about performance.
When you’re designing an e-commerce system, the conversation usually starts like this:

• Customers can place multiple orders.

• Orders contain products.

• Payments belong to orders.

• Shipments belong to orders.
You’re thinking about relationships.
Design Schema ↓Build Features↓Traffic Grows↓Slow Queries?↓Add Indexesbut what if someone asks : “Before you create the table, tell me every way your application will query it.”
That probably feels backwards. But that’s exactly where DynamoDB begins.

If SQL Is So Good, Why Did Amazon Build DynamoDB?
At this point, you might be wondering: “If SQL databases are so mature and indexes work so well, why did Amazon build DynamoDB at all?”
The answer is surprisingly simple.
They weren’t trying to build a faster SQL database.They were solving a different problem.
Imagine you’re Amazon on Prime Day.
Millions of customers are browsing products, placing orders, tracking shipments, and refreshing their order status every few seconds.
At that scale, the challenge isn’t just storing data, it’s serving millions of requests with predictable, low latency as traffic keeps growing.
Relational databases can absolutely scale, but doing so often means adding replicas, sharding data, or carefully planning capacity.
Amazon wanted a database that handled that complexity for you.
SQL Scale Up / Replicas / Shards
│
▼
More Operational Work
DynamoDB Automatic Partitioning
│
▼
Built for Horizontal Scale💡 Key Takeaway
SQL optimizes for flexible querying.
DynamoDB optimizes for predictable performance at massive scale.

So How Does DynamoDB Find Your Data?
Let’s go back to our e-commerce application. Suppose a customer opens the app and taps “My Orders.”
The application needs to fetch all orders for:
CustomerId = C42In PostgreSQL, you don’t usually think about where that data is stored. The database figures it out for you.
But DynamoDB has a different challenge. Imagine your table isn’t sitting on one database server anymore. It’s spread across hundreds of machines.
Now the database has to answer a question before it can even fetch the data:
“Which machine should I ask?”
Scanning every machine would defeat the whole purpose of building a highly scalable database.
There has to be a faster way.

The Partition Key: An Address, Not Just a Column
This is where the Partition Key comes in.
Think of it like the address on a package. Before a courier delivers your parcel, they don’t search every house in the city. They first look at the address to know exactly where to go.
DynamoDB does something very similar.
It takes the Partition Key, runs it through a hashing algorithm, and uses the result to determine which physical partition stores the data.
CustomerId = C42
│
▼
Hash
│
▼
Partition 7
│
▼
Read the DataNotice something important.
The application doesn’t know or care that the data is in “Partition 7.”
It simply provides the partition key, and DynamoDB handles the rest.
💡 Key Takeaway
A Partition Key isn’t just an identifier. It’s how DynamoDB knows where your data lives.
And This Is Why Choosing the Right Key Matters
Imagine you chose Country as your partition key.
India
USA
UK
GermanyNow think about your traffic.
If 80% of your users are from India, then 80% of your requests are likely hitting the same partition.
One partition becomes overloaded while others sit mostly idle.
This is known as a hot partition.
That’s why choosing a partition key isn’t just about uniqueness — it’s about distributing traffic evenly.
Bad Partition Key :India ─────────────► 🔥 Partition A (80% traffic)USA ────────────────► Partition BUK ─────────────────► Partition CThink About It…
Suddenly, DynamoDB’s design starts to make more sense.
The partition key isn’t there because AWS likes introducing new terminology.
It’s there because the database needs a predictable way to locate data across many machines without searching everywhere.
And once you understand that, another question naturally appears.
What if tomorrow the Product team asks us to find orders using OrderId instead of CustomerId?
Can DynamoDB still do that efficiently?
That’s exactly where Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) enter the picture.

The Real Reason DynamoDB Has Indexes
Let’s continue with our Orders table.
We designed it using:

• Partition Key: CustomerId

• Sort Key: OrderTime
Why? Because our application mostly answers questions like:
“Show me all orders for Customer C42.”
Perfect. That’s exactly what we designed for.
Then Product Happens.
A few weeks later, your Product Manager walks over and says:
“Customer support has a new requirement. They need to search directly by Order ID.”
If you’re coming from PostgreSQL, your instinct is immediate.
“No problem. Let’s add an index on OrderId."
That’s a perfectly reasonable answer. In SQL.
But here’s the interesting part.
DynamoDB Has a Bigger Problem. Remember what we learned in the previous section. Our table is physically organized by CustomerId.
CustomerId
│
▼
Hash
│
▼
Physical PartitionSuppose someone now asks:
Find Order = O12345
Notice what’s missing.
There’s no CustomerId.
So…
Which partition should DynamoDB go to?
It doesn’t know.
And if it doesn’t know, the only option is to search every partition. That completely defeats the goal of predictable, single-digit millisecond performance.
This isn’t just an optimization problem anymore. It’s an organization problem.
Looking for OrderId = O12345❓ Partition 1 ?❓ Partition 2 ?❓ Partition 3 ?❓ Partition 4 ?Without knowing the partition key, DynamoDB doesn’t know where to start.
This Is Where a GSI Changes Everything
Instead of making the original table faster…
A Global Secondary Index (GSI) creates another way to organize the same data.
Think of it like a library. Your books are arranged by Author. That’s perfect if someone asks:
“Show me books written by George Orwell.”
But tomorrow someone asks:
“Show me every book published in 2024.”
You have two choices:

• Rearrange the entire library every time someone asks a different question.

• Create another catalog organized by Publication Year.
Libraries choose the second option. So does DynamoDB.
CustomerId
│
▼
Orders
GSIOrderId
│
▼
Same OrdersHere’s the Mental Shift
A SQL index is mostly a shortcut. A DynamoDB GSI is another way of organizing the same data.
Read that again.
That single sente
